content.text: The Enterprise Plan is Claude's top tier, designed for organisations that need corporate identity integration, compliance audit trails, data residency selection, and contractual data protections. Unlike the Team Plan — which is self-serve and starts in minutes — Enterprise requires a sales engagement, a signed contract, and typically a minimum of 40 or more seats.

Most organisations reach Enterprise when one of three requirements appears: IT mandates SSO for all SaaS tools, Legal or Compliance needs an audit log of AI interactions, or Security requires a contractual commitment about where conversation data is processed and stored.

attrs.alt: Claude Enterprise architecture showing identity, access, audit, and data residency components

content.text: The diagram above shows the three integration surfaces that distinguish Enterprise from Team: the corporate identity provider (SSO + SCIM), the audit log export pipeline to SIEM tools, and the data residency region selection. All three require Enterprise-tier contracts; none are configurable in Team or Pro.
Identity Integration: SSO and SCIM
SAML 2.0 Single Sign-On

Enterprise supports SAML 2.0 for single sign-on. Claude acts as the service provider (SP); your corporate identity provider (IdP) — Okta, Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD), Google Workspace, OneLogin, Ping, or any SAML 2.0-compliant system — becomes the authentication authority.

Once SSO is configured, the email/password login path is disabled for your organisation. All authentication flows through your IdP. This means your corporate MFA policies, session length controls, and conditional access rules apply to Claude automatically — without any configuration on the Claude side.
SAML assertion attributes required: NameID (email format), firstName, lastName. Role assignment can optionally be driven by a saml:Attribute element.
SP-initiated flow: member visits claude.ai, enters work email, is redirected to IdP, authenticates, returns to Claude with a valid session.
IdP-initiated flow: member clicks Claude tile in Okta/Entra app launcher, lands in Claude directly without entering email. Available on most enterprise IdPs.
Session lifetime: governed by the IdP's session policy, not Claude's defaults. If your IdP issues 8-hour sessions with MFA re-prompt, Claude sessions follow that policy.
SCIM Provisioning

SCIM (System for Cross-domain Identity Management) is a protocol that lets your IdP automatically create, update, and delete Claude user accounts as your HR system changes. Combined with SSO, SCIM enables a fully automated user lifecycle: a new hire is provisioned within minutes of being added to the correct IdP group, and a departing employee's Claude access is revoked the moment their IdP account is disabled or their group membership is removed.

attrs.alt: SCIM provisioning lifecycle from employee hire to offboard with zero-touch provisioning and automatic deprovisioning

content.text: SCIM provisioning operates on user records and group memberships. The typical configuration maps an IdP group (e.g. 'claude-enterprise-users') to Claude seats, and a second group (e.g. 'claude-admins') to the Admin role. Changes to group membership propagate to Claude within the SCIM sync interval, typically 15–40 minutes depending on IdP configuration.
Configuring SSO with Okta
Okta is one of the most commonly used IdPs. The configuration involves registering Claude as a SAML application in Okta and pasting the metadata URL into the Claude Enterprise Admin Console.

# Okta SAML app configuration (values provided by Anthropic during onboarding)

Single Sign-On URL (ACS URL):  https://claude.ai/auth/saml/callback
Audience URI (SP Entity ID):    https://claude.ai/auth/saml/metadata
Name ID Format:                 EmailAddress
Application username:           Email

# Attribute Statements to configure
Name            Value
----            -----
firstName       user.firstName
lastName        user.lastName
role            (optional) appuser.role   # "admin" or "member"

# SCIM provisioning endpoint (in Okta Provisioning tab)
SCIM Base URL:   https://claude.ai/scim/v2
OAuth Bearer:    <token provided in Claude Admin Console → SSO Settings>
Supported actions: Push Users, Push Groups, Import Users

After saving the Okta configuration, download the IdP metadata XML and upload it in the Claude Enterprise Admin Console under Settings → SSO. Test with a single user account before enabling for the whole organisation.
Audit Logs

Enterprise organisations get access to an immutable audit log that records every significant action taken by admins and members. Audit logs are the primary tool for answering compliance questions: who accessed which Project, when did they run conversations, and what admin changes were made to the organisation.
What Is Logged
Event category
Examples
Authentication
Login (SSO or password), logout, failed login, session expiry
Member management
Invite sent, invite accepted, member removed, role changed, seat deactivated via SCIM
Project operations
Project created, Project settings changed, member added/removed from Project, Project deleted
Knowledge files
File uploaded to Project, file deleted from Project
Conversation activity
Conversation started (Project ID, model used, approximate token count), conversation deleted
Admin config changes
SSO settings changed, data residency changed, audit export configured, API access toggled

Important: audit logs record that a conversation occurred and its metadata (who, when, which Project, which model). They do not record conversation content — the message bodies are not logged to the audit trail. Zero-data retention mode (below) addresses content logging at the infrastructure level.
Exporting Audit Logs
Audit log export is available in two modes from the Admin Console:
UI export:
Download a date-range CSV from the Admin Console. Suitable for ad-hoc compliance reviews or incident investigations. Limited to 90-day export windows.
Streaming export to S3 or GCS:
Configure a cloud storage bucket; Anthropic streams audit events as newline-delimited JSON (NDJSON) files, partitioned by hour. Suitable for SIEM ingestion via Splunk, Datadog, or Elastic.
Audit Log Retention

The default retention period is 12 months. Enterprise contracts can negotiate longer retention windows — common extensions are 24 months for SOC 2 Type II evidence retention or 7 years for financial services regulatory requirements. Retention configuration is set per-organisation in the contract, not in the UI.
Data Residency

Data residency allows Enterprise organisations to choose which geographic region processes their conversation data. This is a contractual and infrastructure-level control, not just a data-routing preference.
Available Regions
Region
Primary use case
Notes
United States (default)
Organisations without geographic requirements
Default for all plans; no configuration required
European Union (Frankfurt)
GDPR compliance, EU data sovereignty requirements
Data processed within EU; DPA addendum required
Asia-Pacific (Singapore)
APAC data sovereignty, latency-sensitive deployments
Available on select Enterprise contracts

Data residency is configured once at the organisation level and applies to all workspaces and conversations. It cannot be mixed — an organisation cannot route some teams to EU and others to US within the same Enterprise account. Separate Enterprise accounts are required if different business units have conflicting residency requirements.
What Data Residency Covers
Conversation content: the text of messages exchanged with Claude is processed and temporarily stored within the selected region.
Knowledge files: documents uploaded to Projects are stored in the selected region.
Audit logs: audit log data is also stored and queryable from within the selected region.
Model inference: Claude inference occurs on infrastructure within the selected region.
What Data Residency Does Not Cover
Account metadata (billing, user records, org configuration) may be stored in Anthropic's primary region regardless of data residency selection.
Anthropic's model weights are not region-specific — the model itself is not stored in your selected region; only the inference workload runs there.
API access via the Anthropic API does not inherit your claude.ai Enterprise data residency settings. API requests are subject to separate API data handling terms.
Zero-Data Retention Mode

Zero-data retention (ZDR) is a high-assurance mode available on Enterprise contracts where Anthropic does not retain conversation content in its logging infrastructure after the response is delivered. This is distinct from the standard no-training guarantee (which Team Plan also provides): ZDR goes further by ensuring the content is not written to Anthropic's persistent storage at all, even transiently for reliability or debugging.

ZDR is primarily relevant for organisations in highly regulated industries — healthcare (HIPAA), finance (FINRA/MiFID II), defence, and legal — where any external retention of client-facing content requires regulatory approval or is prohibited by internal policy.
Availability: ZDR is a contract-level add-on, not a UI setting. It is negotiated during the Enterprise procurement process.
Tradeoff: ZDR limits Anthropic's ability to diagnose errors in your conversations, since no logs exist. Support incidents involving conversation-level bugs are harder to resolve under ZDR.
Compatibility: ZDR applies to claude.ai Enterprise conversations. It does not automatically apply to Anthropic API usage, which has separate terms.
Audit logs under ZDR: metadata (who, when, model) is still logged; only conversation content is not retained. The audit log event records still appear.
Admin Controls
Enterprise admins have substantially more control than Team admins. The Enterprise Admin Console exposes controls that Team Plan omits entirely.
Per-User Usage Controls
Admins can set usage quotas at the user level or the workspace level. A common configuration is a global default with elevated limits for power users:
Default seat quota: standard daily message limit applied to all members.
Elevated quota: a specific list of users (e.g. data science team) granted a higher daily limit.

Model restrictions: admins can restrict which models specific workspaces or user groups can access. If a workspace handles sensitive data and the admin wants to limit to faster, lower-context models for cost control, that restriction can be applied at the workspace level.
Workspace Management

Enterprise organisations can create multiple workspaces — logical boundaries that group Projects, members, and settings. Workspaces allow a single Enterprise account to serve multiple business units without them seeing each other's Projects or conversations.
Feature
Team Plan
Enterprise Plan
Workspaces
One (default)
Multiple — unlimited
Cross-workspace visibility
N/A
None — workspaces are isolated
Workspace-level model controls
Not available
Available
Workspace-level SSO enforcement
Not available
Can set per-workspace SSO policy
Workspace admin delegation
Not available
Org admin can designate workspace admins
Procurement and Contracting
Sales Process

Enterprise does not have a self-serve signup. The process begins at anthropic.com/enterprise or through an Anthropic sales representative. A typical procurement timeline runs 4–12 weeks depending on legal review complexity.
Discovery call: scope seats, workspaces, data residency requirements, compliance certifications needed (SOC 2 Type II report, DPA, BAA).
Technical evaluation: pilot deployment with a limited seat count, SSO configuration testing, audit log export validation.
Legal review: your legal team reviews the Enterprise Service Agreement (ESA), Data Processing Addendum (DPA), and if applicable, Business Associate Agreement (BAA) for HIPAA.
Signature and deployment: agreement signed, Anthropic provisions the Enterprise organisation, SSO configured, SCIM activated, full seat rollout.
Certifications and Compliance Documents
Enterprise customers can request the following from Anthropic:
SOC 2 Type II report: Anthropic's annual security audit report, typically shared under NDA.
Data Processing Addendum (DPA): contractual terms governing how Anthropic processes personal data under GDPR and CCPA.
Business Associate Agreement (BAA): required for HIPAA-covered entities that will use Claude in healthcare workflows.
Penetration test results: available to Enterprise customers under NDA on request.
ISO 27001 certification: check Anthropic's current certification status at time of procurement — certification scope evolves.
Using the Enterprise Admin Console

The Enterprise Admin Console is the central control surface for org admins. It is accessible from the account menu at claude.ai when the logged-in user has the Admin role. The console is distinct from the Team Plan settings UI — Enterprise admins see additional sections that Team admins do not.
Organisation Dashboard

The organisation dashboard shows seat utilisation (active seats vs contracted seats), recent admin activity, and any pending invitations. It does not show per-member conversation counts in real time — usage data appears in the billing section on a monthly basis, not live.
Workspace Setup
Creating a new workspace is a five-step process in the Admin Console:
Navigate to Admin Console → Workspaces → Create Workspace.
Name the workspace to reflect its team or function (e.g. 'Legal', 'Engineering - Platform', 'Customer Success EMEA').
Assign a workspace admin. This person manages Projects and membership within the workspace but cannot change org-level settings.

Set the workspace's model policy: which Claude models members in this workspace may use. Restricting a workspace to Haiku 4.5 only is appropriate for high-volume, latency-sensitive workflows where Opus 4.8 cost is prohibitive.
Add members by email, or enable SCIM group mapping so that IdP group membership auto-populates the workspace.
SSO Configuration Steps
Once the Enterprise contract is signed and Anthropic has provisioned the organisation, SSO configuration takes place in Admin Console → Settings → SSO.
Choose your IdP type from the dropdown (Okta, Entra ID, Google Workspace, Generic SAML 2.0, OIDC).
Copy the SP Entity ID and ACS URL from the Claude console — these go into your IdP's SAML application configuration.
Download or paste your IdP's metadata XML (or enter the IdP SSO URL and certificate fingerprint manually).
Save and run the test connection. Claude sends a test authentication request to your IdP. Verify a successful authentication before enabling for all users.
Enable SSO enforcement. Once enabled, email/password logins are disabled org-wide. Ensure all members have valid IdP accounts before enabling.
Configure SCIM: in Admin Console → Settings → SCIM, generate a bearer token and paste it into your IdP's SCIM provisioning settings. Select 'Push Users' and 'Push Groups'.

Operational tip: keep one backup admin account that does not go through SSO during the SSO rollout. If IdP configuration is wrong and SSO is broken, you need a way to log in and fix it. Anthropic support can temporarily disable SSO for an org, but this requires opening a support ticket and takes time.
Pilot Deployment Checklist

Most Enterprise deployments begin with a pilot — a limited rollout of 10–25 users before full org-wide deployment. A successful pilot validates SSO, SCIM, audit log export, and Project configuration before the full rollout.
Checkpoint
How to verify
Who is responsible
SSO login works
Pilot users can log in via IdP SSO tile. Test both SP-initiated and IdP-initiated flows.
IT admin
SCIM creates accounts
Add a test user to the IdP group; confirm they appear in Claude Admin Console → Members within 40 minutes.
IT admin
SCIM deprovisions accounts
Remove a test user from the IdP group; confirm their Claude seat is deactivated within 40 minutes.
IT admin
Audit log exports
Trigger several conversations; confirm audit events appear in S3 bucket or SIEM within 24 hours.
Security team
Data residency confirmed
Anthropic confirms in writing which region is processing data. Verify DPA addendum specifies the correct region.
Legal / Compliance
Shared Projects work
Pilot users create and join a shared Project; confirm custom instructions apply to all members' conversations.
Business admin
Support escalation tested
Open a test support ticket; confirm it routes to Enterprise support queue with SLA acknowledgement.
IT admin
Cost Considerations

Enterprise pricing is not published — it is negotiated per contract. Several factors influence the per-seat price: total seat count (larger volumes get lower per-seat rates), contract length (annual or multi-year commitments are priced lower than month-to-month), data residency selection (non-US regions may carry a premium), and whether zero-data retention mode is included.
True Cost of Ownership
The visible per-seat subscription cost is not the only cost to account for in an Enterprise deployment:
IT admin time:
SSO and SCIM configuration, ongoing seat reconciliation, support ticket handling. Typically 5–10 hours upfront, 1–2 hours per month ongoing for a 100-seat org.
Legal review:
DPA, BAA, and ESA review. For organisations with strict legal vetting processes, this can add 4–8 weeks to the procurement timeline.
SIEM integration:
If you route audit logs to Splunk, Datadog, or Elastic, the ingestion volume adds to your SIEM costs. Estimate ~50 KB per 1000 conversation events.
Training and adoption:
Enterprise deployments often include a structured rollout — internal training, acceptable-use policy communication, and a help desk knowledge base update. Budget 10–20 hours of project management.
Comparing Total Cost Against Team Plan

For a 50-person team where Team Plan is $25–30/seat/month, the annual Team cost is approximately $15,000–18,000. Enterprise typically comes in higher per seat, but the gap narrows at larger seat counts and when factoring in the SSO administration time that Team Plan admins must spend manually managing access.

The tipping point for Enterprise is usually not purely financial. It appears when a compliance or security gate is hard — when the audit or legal team says 'we cannot use this tool without a signed DPA and audit logs', that is the moment Team Plan becomes insufficient regardless of cost.
Common Enterprise Deployment Patterns
Pattern 1: Department-by-Department Rollout

Start with one department (typically the highest-request team, often Engineering or Marketing) as the pilot. Validate all security controls, then expand one department at a time over 8–12 weeks. Each department gets its own workspace. Advantages: lower risk, manageable support load, early adopters become internal advocates. Disadvantage: slower time to value for the organisation.
Pattern 2: Workload-Scoped Deployment

Deploy Enterprise only for specific high-value or sensitive workflows — legal document review, customer data analysis, security incident triage — while keeping Team Plan for general productivity use. Requires managing two Claude contracts simultaneously but allows tighter data controls where they matter most. IT manages two SSO configurations; audit logs are only required for the Enterprise workload.
Pattern 3: Full Org Deployment with Tiered Model Access

Deploy Enterprise to all staff immediately, but use workspace-level model controls to tier access. Most staff get a workspace with Haiku 4.5 (fast, economical). Power users (data analysts, engineers, legal) get a workspace with Sonnet 4.6 access. A restricted set of researchers gets Opus 4.8 access. This pattern controls cost while maximising access and avoids the complexity of two parallel contracts.
Summary

The Enterprise Plan adds SSO and SCIM identity integration, audit logs with SIEM export, data residency region selection, zero-data retention mode, per-user usage controls, multiple workspaces, and contractual compliance documentation (DPA, BAA, SOC 2) on top of everything the Team Plan provides. The decision to move to Enterprise is almost always driven by one of three requirements: corporate IT mandating SSO for all SaaS, compliance teams needing an audit trail, or legal teams requiring a signed DPA or BAA. All three are only available at the Enterprise tier.
